items.title: Babies Are Born With 300 Bones
items.concept: Mind-blowing fact: babies are born with about 300 bones — nearly 100 more than adults. As they grow, many bones fuse together, leaving adults with just 206. The soft spots on a baby's skull (fontanelles) are where bones haven't fused yet, which is what allows them to fit through the birth canal.
items.hook: Your baby has MORE bones than you do right now.

items.title: Newborns Can Only See 12 Inches Away
items.concept: Newborns can only focus clearly on objects about 8-12 inches from their face — exactly the distance to a parent's face during feeding. Their vision is blurry beyond that. By 3 months they can track moving objects, and by 6 months their color vision is nearly fully developed.
items.hook: Your newborn is basically blind — and it's on purpose.

items.title: Babies Can Breathe and Swallow at the Same Time
items.concept: Babies under 7 months old can breathe and swallow at the same time — something no adult can do. Their larynx sits higher in the throat, creating a separate pathway. This is why babies can nurse continuously without stopping to breathe. They lose this ability as their throat develops for speech.
items.hook: Babies have a superpower that every adult has lost.

items.title: Babies Don't Cry Real Tears at First
items.concept: Newborns cry — a lot — but they don't produce actual tears until they're 2 to 4 weeks old. Their tear ducts aren't fully developed at birth. They can make crying sounds and facial expressions from day one, but the tears come later. Some babies don't produce tears until 2 months old.
items.hook: Your newborn's tears are fake. Literally.

items.title: Why Babies Love Being Shushed
items.concept: The 'shushing' sound that calms babies isn't random — it mimics the constant whooshing noise of blood flow they heard in the womb, which is louder than a vacuum cleaner at about 80-90 decibels. That's why white noise machines work so well. The womb was never silent — it was actually incredibly loud.
items.hook: The womb is louder than a vacuum cleaner.
